preamble.summary: Psalm 45 is a royal wedding psalm — a song composed for the marriage of an Israelite king to a foreign princess. The poet begins by describing the king's beauty, grace, and martial valor (vv. 3-10), then addresses the bride, urging her to forget her homeland and embrace her new identity as the queen (vv. 11-13), and concludes with the procession of the bride into the palace and a promise of royal descendants (vv. 14-18). The superscription calls it a shir yedidot ('a song of love/beloved things'), the only psalm so designated.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 45 is the most explicitly messianic psalm in the Psalter after Psalm 2 and Psalm 110. In verse 7, the king is addressed as Elohim: 'Your throne, O God, is forever and ever' (kis'akha Elohim olam va-ed). Whether Elohim here means 'God' (the king is being addressed as divine or as God's representative) or 'your divine throne' (an attributive use), the verse pushes the Davidic king toward divine status in a way unparalleled in Israelite literature. Hebrews 1:8-9 quotes this verse as proof of the Son's divinity. The psalm's other remarkable feature is its frank celebration of physical beauty, perfume, music, and royal splendor — the ivory palaces, the queen in gold of Ophir, the embroidered garments. This is a psalm that delights in material beauty as an expression of divine blessing, without a trace of ascetic suspicion.
preamble.friction: The address to the king as Elohim (v. 7) is the most debated crux in the psalm. Options include: (1) the king is addressed as 'God' in a court hyperbole reflecting ancient Near Eastern royal ideology; (2) Elohim is vocative but means 'divine one' or 'mighty one' rather than 'God' proper; (3) the text should be repointed to read 'your throne is God's throne' or 'God is your throne.' We retain the traditional vocative reading ('your throne, O God') because the Hebrew syntax most naturally supports it and because the psalm clearly operates within the ideology of the Davidic covenant, where the king is God's adopted son (Psalm 2:7). The identity of the bride is debated — Jezebel (Ahab's marriage), an Egyptian princess (Solomon's era), or a Tyrian princess (the 'daughter of Tyre' in v. 13) are all proposed.
preamble.connections:
The royal ideology connects to 2 Samuel 7 (the Davidic covenant), Psalm 2 (the king as God's son), and Psalm 110 (the priest-king at God's right hand). Hebrews 1:8-9 quotes verses 7-8 as addressed to Christ. The bride's instruction to 'forget your people and your father's house' (v. 11) echoes Genesis 12:1, where Abraham is told to leave his homeland — the bride's journey mirrors the patriarchal call. The 'daughter of Tyre' (v. 13) connects to Solomon's alliance with Hiram of Tyre (1 Kings 5) and to the broader theme of foreign nations bringing tribute to Zion (Isaiah 60:5-7). The myrrh, aloes, and cassia (v. 9) anticipate the spices of Song of Songs.

**Tradition comparisons:** The Latin Vulgate shaped Western theology here: Thronus tuus Deus addresses the king as 'God,' a reading quoted in Hebrews 1:8 as addressed to Christ. This became a key proof-text for Christ's divinity in Latin theology. See the [Vulgate Psalms](/vulgate/psalms).

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֣חַ עַל־שֹׁ֭שַׁנִּים לִבְנֵי־קֹ֑רַח מַ֝שְׂכִּ֗יל שִׁ֣יר יְדִידֹֽת׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Shoshannim, for the sons of Korah, Maschil, A Song of loves.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. Set to 'Lilies.' A maskil of the Sons of Korah. A song of love.
translator_notes: al shoshannim ('set to Lilies') is a musical direction — likely the name of a tune or a reference to lily-shaped instruments. shir yedidot ('a song of beloved things / a song of love') is unique among psalm superscriptions. yedidot is the feminine plural of yadid ('beloved'), making this literally 'a song of beloved-ones' or 'a love song.' The term openly declares the psalm's genre: this is a wedding poem.

verses.text_hebrew: רָ֘חַ֤שׁ לִבִּ֨י ׀ דָּ֘בָ֤ר ט֗וֹב אֹמֵ֣ר אָ֭נִי מַעֲשַׂ֣י לְמֶ֑לֶךְ לְ֝שׁוֹנִ֗י עֵ֣ט סוֹפֵ֥ר מָהִֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: My heart is inditing a good matter: I speak of the things which I have made touching the king: my tongue is the pen of a ready writer.
verses.expanded_rendering: The poet opens with a statement of inspiration. rachash libbi ('my heart stirs/overflows') uses a rare verb that suggests bubbling up, boiling over — the poem is not labored but springs from inner pressure, like a spring forcing itself through rock. davar tov ('a good word/matter') — this is a worthy subject. The poet declares omer ani ma'asai le-melekh ('I speak my works to the king') — this composition is addressed to royalty. The final image is striking: leshoni et sofer mahir ('my tongue is the pen of a skilled scribe'). The poet's tongue moves like the stylus of a professional writer — swift, precise, flowing. This is a claim of inspired craftsmanship.
translator_notes: The verb rachash occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. Cognate languages suggest 'to bubble, to stir, to be in commotion.' The poet's heart is not calmly composing but erupting with creative energy. This self-conscious poetic introduction is rare in the Psalms and signals a court composition of high literary ambition.

verses.text_hebrew: יָפְיָפִ֡יתָ מִבְּנֵ֬י אָדָ֗ם ה֣וּצַק חֵ֭ן בְּשִׂפְתוֹתֶ֑יךָ עַל־כֵּ֤ן בֵּֽרַכְךָ֖ אֱלֹהִ֣ים לְעוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou art fairer than the children of men: grace is poured into thy lips: therefore God hath blessed thee for ever.
translator_notes: yafyafita ('you are exceedingly beautiful') is an unusual pilpel form of yafah ('to be beautiful'), intensifying the adjective — the king is beautiful beyond ordinary beauty. The phrase hutsaq chen be-siftotekha ('grace is poured on your lips') uses the imagery of liquid metal being poured into a mold (yatsaq, 'to pour, to cast'). The king's speech is not merely graceful but divinely crafted. The consequence: al ken berakhekha Elohim le-olam ('therefore God has blessed you forever') — physical beauty and verbal grace are read as evidence of permanent divine favor.

verses.text_kjv: Gird thy sword upon thy thigh, O most mighty, with thy glory and thy majesty.
verses.rendering:
Strap your sword on your thigh, O warrior —
your splendor and your majesty!
translator_notes: The poet shifts from beauty to battle. chagor charbkha al yarekh gibbor ('strap your sword on your thigh, mighty one') addresses the king as a warrior preparing for combat. gibbor ('mighty one, warrior, champion') is one of the divine titles in Isaiah 9:5 (El Gibbor). hodekha va-hadarekha ('your splendor and your majesty') describes the royal warrior's glory — hod and hadar are frequently paired as attributes of God (Psalm 96:6, 104:1), here applied to the king.

verses.text_hebrew: וַהֲדָ֤רְךָ֨ ׀ צְלַ֬ח רְכַ֗ב עַל־דְּבַר־אֱ֭מֶת וְעַנְוָה־צֶ֑דֶק וְתוֹרְךָ֖ נוֹרָא֣וֹת יְמִינֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And in thy majesty ride prosperously because of truth and meekness and righteousness; and thy right hand shall teach thee terrible things.
verses.rendering:
In your majesty, ride out victoriously
for the cause of truth and humility and righteousness.
Let your right hand teach you awesome deeds.
verses.expanded_rendering: The king rides into battle not for conquest alone but for moral causes. al devar emet ve-anvah tsedeq ('for the sake of truth and humility-righteousness') — the three virtues that define the ideal king. emet ('truth, reliability'), anvah ('humility, gentleness'), and tsedeq ('righteousness, justice') are the ethical foundation of royal power. The king's sword serves these values, not personal ambition. torekha noraot yeminekha ('your right hand will teach you awesome/fearsome things') — in battle, the king's own right hand will instruct him through the deeds it performs. The right hand (yemin) is the sword hand, the hand of power.
translator_notes: The combination of military valor with humility (anvah) is surprising. The ideal Israelite king is not merely powerful but meek — a quality that will be central to the messianic portrait in Zechariah 9:9 ('humble, riding on a donkey'). This psalm holds together what later theology tends to separate: the warrior and the servant.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteousness, justice, rightness, what is correct, vindication, right order
key_terms.note: tsedeq and its feminine form tsedaqah define the moral order that the king is charged to uphold. It is not merely personal morality but cosmic rightness — when the king rides for tsedeq, he rides to set the world in order.

verses.text_hebrew: חִצֶּ֗יךָ שְׁ֭נוּנִים עַ֭מִּים תַּחְתֶּ֣יךָ יִפְּל֑וּ בְּ֝לֵ֗ב אוֹיְבֵ֥י הַמֶּֽלֶךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thine arrows are sharp in the heart of the king's enemies; whereby the people fall under thee.
verses.rendering:
Your arrows are sharpened —
peoples fall beneath you —
into the heart of the king's enemies.
translator_notes: The Hebrew syntax is disrupted for dramatic effect: 'your arrows sharpened — peoples beneath you fall — in the heart of the king's enemies.' The middle phrase (peoples fall beneath you) is parenthetical, creating a vivid cinematic effect: arrows fly, nations collapse, and the arrows land in the enemies' hearts. The third-person reference to 'the king' within a second-person address may reflect court protocol.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּסְאֲךָ֣ אֱ֭לֹהִים עוֹלָ֣ם וָעֶ֑ד שֵׁ֥בֶט מִ֝ישֹׁ֗ר שֵׁ֣בֶט מַלְכוּתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever: the sceptre of thy kingdom is a right sceptre.
verses.rendering:
Your throne, O God, is forever and ever;
a scepter of equity is the scepter of your kingdom.
verses.expanded_rendering: The most theologically charged verse in the psalm. kis'akha Elohim olam va-ed ('your throne, O God, is forever and ever') addresses the king with the word Elohim ('God'). The Hebrew syntax most naturally reads as a vocative: the king is being called Elohim. Within the Davidic covenant theology, the king is God's adopted son (2 Samuel 7:14, Psalm 2:7), God's representative on earth, seated on God's own throne (1 Chronicles 29:23 says Solomon 'sat on the throne of the LORD'). The address does not make the king ontologically divine but functionally divine — he exercises God's rule on earth. The scepter (shevet) of this eternal kingdom is characterized by mishor ('uprightness, equity, level ground'). Divine authority exercised with justice — this is the messianic ideal.
translator_notes: Hebrews 1:8-9 quotes this verse as addressed to the Son, making it a foundational christological text. The author of Hebrews reads Elohim as a genuine divine address: the king to whom these words are spoken is, in the fullest sense, God. Whether the original psalmist intended this level of meaning or was working within ancient Near Eastern royal ideology that attributed semi-divine status to kings, the canonical reading — Israel's king as God's own embodiment of rule — reaches toward incarnational theology.

The alternative reading 'your throne is God's, forever and ever' (taking Elohim as a genitive rather than vocative) is grammatically possible but less natural in context. The parallel verse 8 ('therefore God, your God, has anointed you') uses Elohim twice in different senses, suggesting the psalmist is comfortable applying the word both to the king and to the God who anoints him.

key_terms.semantic_range: God, gods, divine being, mighty one, judge
key_terms.note: Elohim as an address to the king is the theological crux of Psalm 45. The word can mean 'God' (the supreme deity), 'gods' (divine beings), 'mighty ones,' or 'judges' (Exodus 21:6, 22:8). Applied to the Davidic king, it reflects the unique status of Israel's monarch as God's representative and adopted son, seated on a throne that is ultimately God's own.

verses.text_hebrew: אָהַ֣בְתָּ צֶּ֭דֶק וַתִּשְׂנָ֣א רֶ֑שַׁע עַל־כֵּ֤ן מְשָׁחֲךָ֨ אֱ֘לֹהִ֤ים אֱלֹהֶ֗יךָ שֶׁ֣מֶן שָׂ֭שׂוֹן מֵחֲבֵרֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou lovest righteousness, and hatest wickedness: therefore God, thy God, hath anointed thee with the oil of gladness above thy fellows.
verses.expanded_rendering: The king's moral character is the ground of his anointing. ahavta tsedeq vatisna resha ('you love righteousness and hate wickedness') — the king's affections are rightly ordered: he loves what is right and hates what is corrupt. Because of this moral alignment, meshacha'kha Elohim Elohekha ('God, your God, has anointed you'). The phrase Elohim Elohekha ('God, your God') distinguishes between the Elohim of verse 7 (the king addressed as God) and the Elohim who anoints him — the king may bear the divine title, but he himself has a God above him. The anointing is shemen sason ('oil of joy/gladness'), the perfumed oil poured on the king's head at coronation — but here specified as joy-oil, celebrating the wedding occasion. me-chavereka ('beyond your companions') — the king is elevated above all his peers.
translator_notes: mashach ('to anoint') is the root of mashiach ('messiah, anointed one'). The king is God's mashiach — anointed with oil as a sign of divine election. The 'oil of joy' (shemen sason) echoes Isaiah 61:3, where God gives 'the oil of joy instead of mourning' — anointing as the reversal of grief.

key_terms.semantic_range: to anoint, to smear with oil, to consecrate, to install in office
key_terms.note: mashach is the act that creates a mashiach ('messiah'). The anointing with oil signifies divine election, empowerment, and consecration for a specific role. Every Davidic king was a mashiach; the expectation of a final, ultimate mashiach grew from this royal anointing tradition.

verses.text_hebrew: מֹר־וַאֲהָל֣וֹת קְ֭צִיעוֹת כׇּל־בִּגְדֹתֶ֑יךָ מִן־הֵ֥יכְלֵ֥י שֵׁ֝֗ן מִנִּ֥י שִׂמְּחֽוּךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: All thy garments smell of myrrh, and aloes, and cassia, out of the ivory palaces, whereby they have made thee glad.
verses.rendering:
Myrrh and aloes and cassia — all your garments.
From ivory palaces, strings make you glad.
translator_notes: The three spices — mor (myrrh), ahalot (aloes), and qetsi'ot (cassia) — are luxury fragrances associated with royalty and love (Song of Songs 4:14). The king's garments are saturated with them. hekhlei shen ('ivory palaces') describe the royal residence — ivory-inlaid buildings like those of Ahab's 'ivory house' (1 Kings 22:39). The word minni (from which) likely introduces musical instruments — strings or harps from these palaces provide the wedding music.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנ֣וֹת מְ֭לָכִים בְּיִקְּרוֹתֶ֑יךָ נִצְּבָ֥ה שֵׁ֝גַ֗ל לִֽימִינְךָ֥ בְּכֶ֣תֶם אוֹפִֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: Kings' daughters were among thy honourable women: upon thy right hand did stand the queen in gold of Ophir.
verses.rendering:
Daughters of kings are among your honored women;
the queen stands at your right hand in gold of Ophir.
verses.expanded_rendering: The scene shifts to the king's court. benot melakhim ('daughters of kings') attend as honored women — foreign princesses are part of the royal entourage, reflecting international alliances. The climactic figure is the shegal ('queen consort'), who stands at the king's right hand — the position of highest honor and intimacy. She is dressed be-khetem Ofir ('in gold of Ophir'), the finest gold known to the ancient world. Ophir was a legendary source of gold, possibly in Arabia, East Africa, or India (1 Kings 9:28, 10:11). The queen's golden attire signals that she has been elevated to the highest status the court can bestow.
translator_notes: shegal ('queen, consort') is an Aramaic loanword that appears rarely in Hebrew (here and in Nehemiah 2:6). It denotes the queen consort — the king's primary wife, distinct from secondary wives or concubines. Her position at the king's right hand mirrors the position of the king himself at God's right hand (Psalm 110:1).

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁמְעִי־בַ֣ת וּ֭רְאִי וְהַטִּ֣י אׇזְנֵ֑ךְ וְשִׁכְחִ֥י עַ֝מֵּ֗ךְ וּבֵ֥ית אָבִֽיךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Hearken, O daughter, and consider, and incline thine ear; forget also thine own people, and thy father's house;
verses.rendering:
Listen, daughter, and see, and turn your ear:
forget your people and your father's house.
verses.expanded_rendering: The poet now addresses the bride directly. shim'i vat ur'i ve-hatti oznekh ('listen, daughter, and look, and incline your ear') — three imperatives demanding her full attention. The instruction is radical: shikhchi ammekh u-vet avikh ('forget your people and your father's house'). She must leave behind her identity, her homeland, her family of origin. This echoes God's command to Abraham in Genesis 12:1 (lekh lekha me-artsekha u-mi-moladetekha u-mi-beit avikha). The bride's journey to the Israelite court mirrors the patriarch's journey to the promised land — both require severing the old life to begin the new. The word bat ('daughter') is tender — the poet speaks as a father or counselor to a young woman entering a new world.
translator_notes: The demand to 'forget your people' is not cruel but liturgical — the bride is entering a covenant that requires total reorientation. In the ancient Near East, a royal bride who married into a foreign court was expected to adopt the religion and customs of her new kingdom. The psalmist makes this expectation explicit.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיִתְאָ֣ו הַמֶּ֣לֶךְ יׇפְיֵ֑ךְ כִּי־ה֥וּא אֲ֝דֹנַ֗יִךְ וְהִשְׁתַּחֲוִי־לֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: So shall the king greatly desire thy beauty: for he is thy Lord; and worship thou him.
verses.rendering:
Then the king will desire your beauty,
for he is your lord — bow before him.
translator_notes: yit'av ha-melekh yofyekh ('the king will desire your beauty') — the verb avah ('to desire, to long for') expresses the king's attraction to the bride. The statement ki hu adonayikh ('for he is your lord') establishes the marital authority of the husband in the ancient context. ve-hishtachavi lo ('and bow to him') — the bride is instructed to show reverence to the king, just as subjects bow before their sovereign. The dual role of husband and lord reflects the ancient Near Eastern fusion of domestic and political authority.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבַת־צֹ֨ר בְּ֭מִנְחָה פָּנַ֣יִךְ יְחַלּ֑וּ עֲשִׁ֖ירֵ֣י עָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And the daughter of Tyre shall be there with a gift; even the rich among the people shall intreat thy favour.
verses.rendering:
The daughter of Tyre comes with a gift;
the wealthy of the people seek your favor.
translator_notes: bat Tsor ('the daughter of Tyre') may refer to the city of Tyre personified, or to a Tyrian princess. Tyre was the premier commercial power of the eastern Mediterranean — its presence with tribute signals that the queen commands international respect. The phrase ashire am ('the wealthy of the people') seek her face (panayikh yechallu, literally 'they entreat your face'). The bride has become a figure of power.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־כְּבוּדָּ֣ה בַת־מֶ֣לֶךְ פְּנִ֑ימָה מִֽמִּשְׁבְּצ֖וֹת זָהָ֣ב לְבוּשָֽׁהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The king's daughter is all glorious within: her clothing is of wrought gold.
verses.rendering:
All glorious is the king's daughter within the palace;
her garment is woven with gold.
verses.expanded_rendering: kol kevuddah bat melekh penimah ('all the glory of the king's daughter is within') — penimah can mean 'inwardly' (her glory is internal, spiritual) or 'within the palace' (she is inside the royal chambers, being prepared for the procession). The double meaning may be intentional: she is glorious both in character and in setting. Her garment (levushah) is mi-mishbetsot zahav ('of gold settings/interweavings') — the word mishbetsot refers to checkered work or filigree settings, the same term used for the gold settings of the priestly breastplate (Exodus 28:11). The bride's attire has priestly echoes.
translator_notes: kevuddah (feminine of kavod, 'glory') is used of the bride. kavod is typically reserved for God's glory or the glory of kings — its application to the bride elevates her to a status parallel with royalty and divinity. The gold-woven garment connects to the high priest's ephod, creating a subtle link between the queen and the priestly office.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, honor, weight, splendor, dignity, magnificence
key_terms.note: kavod (masculine) / kevuddah (feminine) denotes weighty significance — the visible manifestation of inner worth. Applied to the bride, it confers on her the same gravitas that the Hebrew Bible typically reserves for God and kings.

verses.text_hebrew: לִרְקָמ֗וֹת תּוּבַ֥ל לַמֶּ֑לֶךְ בְּתוּל֥וֹת אַ֝חֲרֶ֗יהָ רֵ֘עוֹתֶ֥יהָ מוּבָא֥וֹת לָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: She shall be brought unto the king in raiment of needlework: the virgins her companions that follow her shall be brought unto thee.
translator_notes: The bridal procession moves from the preparation chambers to the king's presence. lirqamot ('in embroidered work') describes the ornate garments. betulot achareyha re'oteyha ('virgins behind her, her companions') form the bridal retinue — young women who attend the bride in her procession. The shift from third person ('she is led') to second person ('brought to you') brings the reader suddenly into the king's perspective.

verses.text_hebrew: תּוּבַ֥לְנָה בִשְׂמָחֹ֣ת וָגִ֑יל תְּ֝בֹאֶ֗ינָה בְּהֵ֣יכַל מֶֽלֶךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: With gladness and rejoicing shall they be brought: they shall enter into the king's palace.
verses.rendering:
They are led with gladness and rejoicing;
they enter the king's palace.
translator_notes: Verse 16 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּ֣חַת אֲ֭בֹתֶיךָ יִהְי֣וּ בָנֶ֑יךָ תְּשִׁיתֵ֥מוֹ לְ֝שָׂרִ֗ים בְּכׇל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: Instead of thy fathers shall be thy children, whom thou mayest make princes in all the earth.
verses.rendering:
In place of your fathers will be your sons;
you will appoint them as princes throughout the earth.
translator_notes: The address returns to the king. tachat avotekha yihyu vanekha ('in place of your fathers will be your sons') — the dynasty will continue, the sons replacing the ancestors as rulers. teshitemo le-sarim be-khol ha-arets ('you will set them as princes in all the earth') extends the dynasty's rule beyond Israel to universal dominion. This is Davidic covenant language at its most expansive, pointing toward the messianic vision of worldwide rule.

verses.text_hebrew: אַזְכִּ֣ירָה שִׁ֭מְךָ בְּכׇל־דֹּ֣ר וָדֹ֑ר עַל־כֵּ֥ן עַמִּ֥ים יְ֝הוֹדֻ֗ךָ לְעֹלָ֥ם וָעֶֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: I will make thy name to be remembered in all generations: therefore shall the people praise thee for ever and ever.
verses.rendering:
I will cause your name to be remembered in every generation;
therefore the peoples will praise you forever and ever.
translator_notes: The poet's claim to immortalize the king's name is itself a form of prophetic speech — the psalm has in fact preserved this king's memory for three thousand years. The shift from 'your sons will rule' (v. 17) to 'peoples will praise you forever' (v. 18) moves from dynastic politics to cosmic worship, making the psalm a bridge between history and eschatology.
